findings.title: readAllData's new endOffsets parameter overstates its behavior
findings.why: The new `startOffsets, endOffsets` pair reads like an enforced byte-range contract, but only `startOffsets` actually controls I/O. `endOffsets` is used only to size concurrency and log estimated work, so the name suggests stronger behavior than the helper provides.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: Future callers can reasonably assume `readAllData` stops at `endOffsets` and wire follow-up changes around that assumption, which makes this helper easy to misuse as the API spreads to more call sites.
findings.evidence: In `readAllData`, `endOffsets` is referenced only in the sizing loop, while the actual reader call passes just `startOffsets[fileIdx]` into `readOneFile` and range enforcement still depends on key filtering.
findings.change_request: Prefer explicit naming here; current `endOffsets` name is confusing. Rename it to something like `estimatedEndOffsets`, or add a short contract comment that `startOffsets` are seek positions and `endOffsets` are sizing hints only.

findings.title: getReadRangeFromProps exposes an opaque tuple-shaped contract
findings.why: This helper now sits on a shared internal surface, but its return type `[][2][]uint64` does not communicate what each index means. The caller has to recover intent from `[0]` and `[1]` conventions instead of named fields, and the `jobKeys` parameter leaks one caller's context into a helper reused elsewhere.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: The contract becomes harder to review and easier to wire incorrectly, especially because the helper is already reused from engine code, merge code, tests, and benchmarks.
findings.evidence: The helper signature introduces `jobKeys [][]byte` and returns `[][2][]uint64`, then callers unpack it with positional indexing such as `readRanges[0][0]` and `readRanges[1][1]` in `merge_v2.go`, `engine.go`, and test code.
findings.change_request: Prefer a named result shape here; current tuple surface is confusing. A small struct such as `type ReadRange struct { StartOffsets []uint64; EndOffsets []uint64 }` plus a neutral parameter name like `boundaryKeys` would make the contract self-describing.
